name: Hospital de Sang and Turó de la Cort Fosca Circular Trail
name_original: Alzina del Salari, Balma dels Traginers, Hospital de Sang – Ruta Circular
description: Hike a moderate 3.9-mile loop through Sant Llorenç del Munt i l'Obac Natural Park, exploring caves, historic sites, and panoramic views.
region_intro: Hiking around Mura offers diverse terrain within the Parc Natural de Sant Llorenç del Munt i l'Obac, characterized by its mountainous landscape, forests, and unique rock formations. The region features a network of trails that traverse hills and valleys, providing varied elevation changes. Natural features include ancient woodlands, rocky outcrops, and scenic viewpoints overlooking the surrounding area. Best hiking trails around Mura The most popular hiking route is Circular Els Tres Monts des de Mura, a 5.4 miles (8.7 km) trail that takes 3 hours 18 minutes to complete, offering a moderate challenge through varied landscapes. Another top favourite among local hikers is Hospital de Sang and Turó de la Cort Fosca Circular Trail, a moderate 3.9 miles (6.3 km) path, winding through forests and past historical sites. Local hikers also love the Mura – Puig de la Balma loop from Mura, a 3.1 miles (5.0 km) trail leading through the local countryside, often completed in about 1 hour 46 minutes. Hiking around Mura is defined by mountainous terrain, dense woodlands, and scenic viewpoints, with options for different ability levels from easy walks to more challenging ascents. The routes in Mura are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 330 reviews. More than 3000 hikers have used komoot to explore Mura's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Mura?
A: Mura offers a wide variety of hiking experiences with over 120 routes. These trails cater to different skill levels, including 2 easy routes, 68 moderate routes, and 53 more challenging options.

Q: What kind of terrain and natural features can I expect while hiking in Mura?
A: Hiking in Mura primarily takes you through the Parc Natural de Sant Llorenç del Munt i l'Obac. The terrain is mountainous, featuring dense woodlands, unique rock formations, and varied elevation changes. You'll encounter ancient forests, rocky outcrops, and scenic viewpoints overlooking the surrounding landscape.

Q: Are there any easy hikes suitable for beginners in Mura?
A: Yes, Mura has several easy routes perfect for beginners or those looking for a relaxed walk. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5333386" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mura – Puig de la Balma loop from Mura&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 3.1-mile (5.0 km) trail that can be completed in under two hours, offering a pleasant journey through the local countryside.

Q: Can I find circular hiking routes in Mura?
A: Yes, circular routes are a popular option in Mura. A notable example is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e950011727/circular-els-tres-monts-des-de-mura" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Circular Els Tres Monts des de Mura&lt;/a&gt;, a 5.4-mile (8.7 km) trail that offers a moderate challenge through varied landscapes. Another favorite is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e938985952/hospital-de-sang-and-turo-de-la-cort-fosca-circular-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hospital de Sang and Turó de la Cort Fosca Circular Trail&lt;/a&gt;, a 3.9-mile (6.3 km) path winding through forests and past historical sites.

Q: Are there any specific landmarks or points of interest to see along the trails?
A: Mura's trails are rich with natural and historical landmarks. You can explore various peaks like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/423435" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Mola and the Monastery of Sant Llorenç del Munt&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/423431" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montcau Summit&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features interesting caves, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1588289" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Blood Hospital&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2857414" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cau dels Emboscats Cave&lt;/a&gt;, offering unique exploration opportunities.

Q: Is Mura a good destination for family-friendly hikes?
A: Yes, Mura offers several routes suitable for families, particularly the easier and moderate trails. The diverse landscape and points of interest like caves and viewpoints can make for an engaging experience for children. Always check the specific trail's length and elevation to ensure it matches your family's capabilities.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the hiking trails in Mura?
A: Many trails in the Parc Natural de Sant Llorenç del Munt i l'Obac are dog-friendly, allowing you to enjoy the natural beauty with your canine companion. However, it's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or other hikers, and to check for any specific local regulations or protected areas where restrictions might apply.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Mura?
A: The best time to hike in Mura is generally during spring (April to June) and autumn (September to November) when the weather is mild and the natural scenery is particularly vibrant. Summers can be hot, making early morning or late afternoon hikes preferable, while winters offer a different, often quieter, experience, though some higher trails might be colder.

Q: What do other hikers say about their experience in Mura?
A: The komoot community highly rates hiking in Mura, with an impressive average score of 4.6 stars from over 330 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the region's varied terrain, the beauty of its dense woodlands, and the rewarding scenic viewpoints. Over 3000 hikers have used komoot to explore Mura's trails, highlighting its popularity.

Q: Are there any trails in Mura that feature waterfalls?
A: While the primary natural features of Mura are its mountainous terrain, forests, and rock formations, the broader region of Catalonia, where Mura is located, does have areas known for waterfalls. For trails specifically featuring waterfalls, you might need to explore slightly further afield from the immediate Mura area, as the local guide data does not explicitly list waterfalls within the immediate hiking routes.

Q: What are some of the more challenging hikes in the Mura region?
A: For experienced hikers seeking a challenge, Mura offers several difficult routes. One such option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10487905" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Mola (1,103 m) – Morral del Drac loop from Parc Natural de Sant Llorenç del Munt i l'Obac&lt;/a&gt;, a 13.6 km trail with significant elevation changes. Another challenging route is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1472758" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montcau - La Mola – Simanya Petita Cave loop from Parc Natural de Sant Llorenç del Munt i l'Obac&lt;/a&gt;, which is 4.6 km but rated difficult due to its terrain.

Q: Are there options for public transport to reach the trailheads in Mura?
A: While Mura is a small village, public transport options to reach the area exist, though they might require some planning. It's advisable to check local bus schedules from larger nearby towns or cities that connect to Mura. For specific trailhead access, a combination of public transport and a short walk or taxi might be necessary.

Q: Where can I find parking for hiking trails in Mura?
A: Parking is generally available in and around the village of Mura, particularly near popular trailheads or designated parking areas for the Parc Natural de Sant Llorenç del Munt i l'Obac. It's recommended to arrive early, especially on weekends or holidays, as spaces can fill up quickly. Always look for official parking signs and respect local regulations.
tips: This car park is free and has a capacity of about 53 vehicles. But it fills up very quickly and you have to get up early to be able to park there. The name of this place is due to the fact that there was a large oak tree 13 meters high that died in 1985. For the second part of the name, sal lari or salari (salary in Spanish), there are two theories: the first is that salt ·lari was the surname of a person who had a shack next to the tree. And the other theory is that next to the tree is where the wages of the woodcutters of the site were paid.

It is a small cave 4.50 meters long and 1.50 meters high. It is located on the left side of the road and to enter you have to bend down a little. There is no documentation of the cave and its use is unknown.

This cave is one of the many vestiges that remain from the passage of muleteers through the park. It was used as a shelter for the muleteers themselves and as a resting place with short stays for pack animals on their long journey to more distant places.
Great views towards Montserrat. We came from the direction of Alina del Sallari and were quite overwhelmed by the sudden wide view.

The Alzina del Vent, also known as the "Bella Alzina" or "Alzina de la Coma d'en Vila", has a height of about 12 meters and a trunk with a large 2.8 meter perimeter.

The main attraction of this holm oak is its numerous branches, all thick, firm and long, that give it a monumental aspect.

One of the great connoisseurs of Sant Llorenç del Munt and l'Obac, Antoni Ferrando i Roig, says that "this is the best formed holm oak in the whole mass". Perhaps this is the reason that has allowed it to become a centenary, delivering on the logging into which until the end of the 20th century the extensive holm-oak trees that were present everywhere in the masses were exposed to make charcoal and firewood.

Antoni Ferrando also explains that the broad shadow generated by the size of his bracam was very sought after by pastors at midday to protect themselves and their flocks from the strength of the sun.

In December 2010, the Diputació de Barcelona, as the manager of the Natural Park, agreed to prune the dead branches of the oak and, in addition, to treat it against various pests.

La Alzina Bonica is one of the many places in the massif, where every year at Christmas the hiker groups place cribs.

(Source: http://trailsantllorenc.blogspot.com.es/2011/01/lalzina-del-vent-891-m.html#.WezYYGi0NhE)
Balma q allows you to sit and shelter from the rain ....

The so-called Blood Hospital is a large triangular ravine that crosses in the middle (as if it were a natural tunnel) a rocky end of the Turó de la Fosca. Its current name comes from the 19th century, when this cavity was used by the Carlist army as a hospital to care for its wounded. It was later a refuge for woodcutters and charcoal burners, but has survived its name of Carlist origin.
To condition the cavity, the soldiers covered its two accesses (on either side of the rock) with exposed masonry walls, part of which is still preserved.
The Carlists chose this place for its size, for having a water source (Rossinyol's fountain located in the neighboring cave of the Cort Fosca) and for being strategically hidden and offering an excellent aerial view of the Torrent del Figueret, the ridge of the Espluga and other parts of the Obac.

You only have to deviate very little from the path to see the cave (it is for caving and is about 340 meters long). It is called Picarol, rattlesnake in Catalan, because it is said that the remains of an animal were found that still wore the rattlesnake around its neck.

faqs.question: Where does the trail start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The route typically starts at the parking area named after L'Alzina del Sal·lari, which is a historical starting point for excursions in the area. You can find this location and directions on Komoot: L'Alzina del Sal·lari.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners or families with young children?
faqs.answer: This is a moderate hike, requiring good physical condition and steady footing. While many paths are accessible, some sections involve walking on bare rock or irregular terrain. It might be a bit long for very young children, and some hiking experience is recommended.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this hike?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter varied terrain, including accessible paths, sections on bare rock, and narrow channels. The region is known for its conglomerate rock formations, leading to numerous natural concavities or 'balmas'. You'll also walk through forested and shady areas, often following ancient cattle routes.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail features several notable sites. You'll pass by the historical Hospital de Sang, a large natural cavity used by Carlist soldiers in the 19th century: The Blood Hospital. You'll also see impressive natural rock formations, various 'balmas' like the Graons Rock Shelter (Mura), and majestic trees such as The Holm Oak of the Wind: The Holm Oak of the Wind. The route also offers panoramic views, including the Montserrat mountain range.

faqs.question: Is the Alzina del Salari, Balma dels Traginers, Hospital de Sang – Circular Route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are allowed on trails within the Parc Natural de Sant Llorenç del Munt i l'Obac. However, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure their safety, especially in areas with varied terrain or near historical sites.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to hike this circular route?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed during most seasons. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery, making them ideal. Summer can be hot, so an early start is advisable, while winter hikes are possible but check weather conditions for potential cold or wet sections.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to hike in Parc Natural de Sant Llorenç del Munt i l'Obac?
faqs.answer: No, there are typically no permits or entrance fees required to access the trails within the Parc Natural de Sant Llorenç del Munt i l'Obac. However, always respect park regulations, such as staying on marked paths and not disturbing wildlife or historical sites.

faqs.question: Is the trail well-marked, or do I need navigation tools?
faqs.answer: A significant portion of this itinerary may not be signposted. Therefore, it is highly recommended to carry a map or use a GPS device, such as the Komoot app, especially if you are unfamiliar with the area, to ensure you stay on track.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other hiking trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other trails. Notable sections include parts of El Camí IT7TR1 El Camí de Llevant: de Montserrat a Salses, Sender dels Miradors, and GR 5. You'll also cross sections of the Camí ramader del Vallès and Camí Ramader i Camí Ral de Mura i Rocafort.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this hike?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and varied terrain, you should bring sturdy hiking shoes, sufficient water, snacks, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and layers of clothing suitable for changing weather conditions. A map or GPS device is also essential for navigation.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities or places to eat near the trailhead?
faqs.answer: While the immediate trailhead at Alzina del Salari is primarily a parking area, the nearby towns and villages in the Sant Llorenç del Munt i l'Obac Natural Park area offer various services, including cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options. It's a good idea to check in advance for specific opening times.
